old_claim.note: Theater Ticketing Systems provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The first public opera house depended on paid-entry theater commerce: San Cassiano opened opera to a paying audience through entrance-ticket practice.

source_shape.source_locator: Our History states San Cassiano was the first public opera theatre in the sense of opening to a paying audience and says purchasing an entrance ticket by each spectator established public-opera commerce in 1637.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source documents entrance-ticket purchase and paying-audience operation as the commercial basis of public opera at San Cassiano.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Ticketing made the venue public and commercial, so the edge is a scaling/business dependency rather than a generic technical enabler.

ontology_before: Opera House Infrastructure depended on Theater Ticketing Systems as an unsourced generic enabler.
ontology_after: Opera House Infrastructure depends on Theater Ticketing Systems as the source-backed paid-entry commercial model for public opera.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: theater_ticketing_systems remains direct but is typed commercial_or_scaling_dependency with review evidence.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to private court opera houses only.
A stronger source showed the first public opera house did not use paid-entry audience commerce.
Theater Ticketing Systems were narrowed away from paid admission or seating-control practice.
short_reason: San Cassiano's public-opera model depended on entrance-ticket commerce.
